query: evidence on helmet cells in hemolytic uremic syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Most biopsies performed on patients with HUS occurred prior to 1990 because patients with suspected HUS are not routinely biopsied due to the presence of thrombocytopenia and general instability. In biopsies that were performed, light microscopy revealed fragmented RBC in glomerular capillary loops and variable fibrin staining in glomerular capillaries and renal arterioles. Electron microscopy shows endothelial and mesangial cell deposits of fibrin and proteinaceous material pushing into the capillary lumen giving the appearance of a glomerular basement membrane with a double contour. In typical STEC-associated HUS, staining for C1q, C3, or C4 is not observed.(doi: 10.1007/978-3-662-52972-0_26 )

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Thrombotic microangiopathy encompasses various systemic diseases in which endothelial damage causes thrombosis in the microvasculature, including capillaries, arterioles, and venules, resulting in consumptive platelet aggregations. This leads to mechanical shearing of the red blood cells (RBCs), Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia, and end-organ damage. The triad of thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia, and ischemic end-organ damage defines thrombotic microangiopathy. [2] [3] Some of the more common TMAs from which HUS must be differentiated are thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP); syndrome of hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes and low platelets (HELLP); and disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). Despite similar pathogeneses, the treatments of these entities differ significantly. [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to cytotoxic effects, Shiga toxin is capable of activating the complement system by inhibiting complement factor H. Upon entering the bloodstream, Shiga toxin persists in binding to cells via the Gb3 receptor, with the highest prevalence found in the glomerular microvasculature. Endothelial damage is caused by 1) direct cytotoxicity of Shiga toxin, 2) disturbance of the hemostatic pathway, 3) increased cytokine release, and 4) alternative pathway activation. [3] This endothelial damage then initiates the pathology associated with TMA.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Anemia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: When examining a peripheral blood smear under a microscope, specific findings can provide valuable insights into the underlying condition. Observations related to certain types of anemia are as follows: Microangiopathic hemolysis : In conditions characterized by microangiopathic hemolysis, such as TTP, HUS, and DIC, a peripheral blood smear examination may reveal several abnormal RBC shapes. These abnormalities include helmet cells, fragmented RBCs (schistocytes), and other RBC fragments. Autoimmune hemolytic anemias or hereditary spherocytosis : Spherocytes (small, round RBCs lacking central pallor) may be present in some cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Up to 20% of patients have elevated amylase and lipase due to pancreatic damage, which may be accompanied by hyperglycemia.(doi: 10.1007/978-3-662-52972-0_26 ) A stool sample should be collected to test for Shiga toxin whenever diarrhea is present. The results may not be positive for the Shiga toxin if it has been cleared or is avidly bound to the endothelium. If clinically indicated, testing for the presence of S dysenteriae and S pneumonia should also be performed.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In HTRs, a peripheral blood smear will often reveal evidence of hemolysis, which may include the presence of keratocytes, helmet cells, bite cells, blister cells, spherocytes, or microspherocytes. These abnormal RBC shapes and forms are indicative of the destruction of RBCs that can occur in such reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: HUS is typically associated with bacterial infection resulting from the consumption of undercooked beef, unpasteurized milk, or other food or drink contaminated by cattle manure; cattle are asymptomatic carriers of STEC. [3] Once ingested, STEC penetrates the mucous layer of the intestine and secretes Shiga toxin, which binds to the receptor Gb3. The Shiga toxin/Gb3 complex binds to cell ribosomes, inhibiting protein synthesis and causing apoptosis; inflammatory cytokines are also produced. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: STEC-HUS incidence is estimated at 0.57 cases per 100,000 children, and in the highest risk group—children aged 6 months to 2 years—the incidence is as high as 3 per 100,000 children. [3] Incidence directly coordinates with environmental exposure and agricultural practices, such as cattle raising, and most patients are diagnosed between April and September when cattle show higher colonization of STEC. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Prior classifications of HUS often depended on the presence or absence of bloody diarrhea, with its presence used to diagnose typical HUS associated with Shiga toxin. However, atypical HUS can also present with bloody diarrhea in up to 30% of cases, so an etiology-based classification system is preferred. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The management of typical HUS caused by STEC is generally supportive. Patients are often volume depleted, and less volume resuscitation has been linked to an increased need for renal replacement therapy, which half of all patients require. [3] [13] Blood transfusions are provided as clinically needed, and platelet transfusions should be given sparingly to avoid thrombotic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS) is a rare but potentially serious condition, often due to infection and less commonly to genetic predisposition or other underlying conditions. Prompt recognition of HUS's varied etiologies and manifestations is essential for timely diagnosis and intervention, optimizing patient outcomes. This activity describes the evaluation of hemolytic uremic syndrome and how to differentiate HUS from other forms of thrombotic microangiopathies. The differing etiologies of HUS, varying clinical presentations, and cellular pathways are reviewed. The roles of the interprofessional healthcare team and regulatory bodies in implementing best practices for treating and preventing this disease to improve patient outcomes are presented.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The cause of aHUS is the abnormal activation of the alternative complement pathway. The complement system is part of the innate immune system and is composed of 3 pathways of activation: classical, alternative, and lectin-binding. The initial steps of each separate pathway converge at the step of forming C3 convertase, leading to the formation of the membrane-attack complex (MAC), which then lyses target cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterohemorrhagic Escherichia coli -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the risk of Coombs-negative MAHA, a hemolysis screen is warranted in cases of anemia. A blood film may reveal red cell fragmentation, and hypocomplementemia may occur. ADAMST13 does not typically decrease in EHEC HUS, and a reduction in ADAMST13 is more commonly associated with aHUS. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Introduction -- Atypical HUS
retrieved_docs.content: Atypical HUS (aHUS) constitutes 5% to 10% of HUS cases and is linked to genetic mutations affecting the alternative complement pathway. Under physiological conditions, the alternative complement system is continuously active at low levels. However, inflammatory conditions such as infections can induce endothelial damage, triggering the activation of the coagulation cascade and causing a TMA presentation. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The course of aHUS has traditionally been much less benign than typical HUS; however, the advent of eculizumab treatment has decreased progression to ESRD or death in children from 30% to 50% down to 9% and in adults from 60% to 6% down to 15%. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2888
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with anemia (فقر الدم), as the sudden loss of renal function often leads to reduced production of erythropoietin and impaired clearance of uremic toxins, which together contribute to a decrease in red blood cell count. This relationship highlights the importance of monitoring hemoglobin levels in patients with acute renal injury and addressing anemia promptly to improve oxygen delivery, reduce cardiovascular strain, and support overall recovery. Effective management of both conditions is essential for optimizing patient outcomes in the setting of acute kidney dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 740
retrieved_docs.content: central concept linked to hypertension, specific renal syndromes (hemolytic‑uremic syndrome, lupus nephritis, crescentic glomerulonephritis), procedural interventions, and broader interpretations of impairment, emphasizing the importance of integrated diagnostic coding, etiologic understanding, and multidisciplinary management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2812
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hereditary hemoglobin disorder that can precipitate serious respiratory complications in adults. Clinically, داء الكريات المنجلية is associated with respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and adult cardiac arrest, underscoring the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt intervention in patients affected by this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 133
retrieved_docs.content: is noted for lacking an autoimmune mechanism despite its inclusion in the broader immune‑disorder framework. Thalassemia is also associated clinically with sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية). Collectively, these entities illustrate that various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, especially those within the “other deficiency anemia” group, share immunologic associations and frequently intersect with chronic kidney disease and other immune‑mediated conditions, highlighting the need for coordinated hematologic, immunologic, and nephrologic assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2038
retrieved_docs.content: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية) is clinically associated with hypocalcemia (نقص كالسيوم الدم), indicating that patients with this immune‑mediated destruction of red blood cells may also exhibit low blood calcium levels. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it suggests that calcium monitoring and appropriate management of hypocalcemia should be considered when evaluating and treating individuals with autoimmune hemolytic anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1392
retrieved_docs.content: Prednison may be used to treat فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية (autoimmune hemolytic anemia). By suppressing the immune response, prednison reduces the production of auto‑antibodies that target red blood cells, thereby decreasing hemolysis and improving anemia. This therapeutic option is important for managing patients whose red blood cell destruction is driven by an autoimmune process.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 934
retrieved_docs.content: that warrant careful ophthalmologic monitoring in patients with thalassemic disorders. Overall, the interrelationships among chronic kidney disease–related anemia, various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, thalassemia, and related immune‑mediated conditions underscore the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies that address both hematologic abnormalities and their associated systemic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2093
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, also referred to by its Arabic name داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hematologic disorder that can clinically manifest as adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, or arrest. This respiratory complication is classified under the broader category of respiratory disorders (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ), which themselves are subtypes of asphyxia (الاِختِنَاق). The disease’s manifestation of low blood‑oxygen levels (نقص الأكسجين في الدم) reflects the same pathophysiologic process that defines asphyxia. Consequently, sickle cell anemia is linked to hypoxemia, which underlies the development of respiratory failure, situating the condition within a hierarchical framework that progresses from the specific hematologic disease to respiratory disorder and ultimately to the general concept of asphyxia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1800
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is identified as a specific subtype of فقر الدم (anemia), indicating that it falls under the broader category of anemia disorders. In clinical practice, فقر الدم (anemia) is frequently associated with متلازمة فانكوني (Fanconi syndrome), a renal tubular disorder that can lead to impaired reabsorption of nutrients and electrolytes, contributing to the development of anemia. Consequently, patients with Fanconi syndrome may present with various forms of anemia, including acute posthemorrhagic anemia when hemorrhagic events occur, highlighting the importance of recognizing the link between renal tubular dysfunction and hematologic abnormalities for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1830
retrieved_docs.content: اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (neonatal disorder) is a broad clinical category that includes specific conditions such as respiratory distress syndrome and كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية (excess fetal red erythroblasts). The inverse_isa relationship indicates that respiratory distress syndrome is a subtype of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, while the has child relationship shows that كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية is a manifestation or sub‑condition within the same neonatal disorder. For each of these entities—اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, respiratory distress syndrome, and كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية—the qualifier “etiologie” is allowed, meaning that the cause or etiology can be specified for each condition. Recognizing and describing the etiological factors of the neonatal disorder and its constituent conditions is essential for accurate diagnosis and targeted management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 0 -- Community 7
retrieved_docs.content: hemolytic, and hemorrhagic disorders are hierarchically organized and interrelated, providing a structured framework for diagnosis, classification, and patient‑centered management during the critical perinatal and neonatal periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 0 -- Community 17
retrieved_docs.content: growth restriction, Rh‑mediated hemolytic disease, diverse neonatal hemorrhagic and jaundice disorders, perinatal infections, and birth trauma are interrelated, underscoring the need for integrated assessment, prevention of delivery trauma and infection, and specialized care for extremely preterm infants to improve outcomes across the fetal, neonatal, and early infant periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2612
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, also referred to by its Arabic name داء الكريات المنجلية, is classified as a hemoglobinopathy. In the context of this disease, respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is identified as a clinical complication, and the Arabic term الاِختِنَاق (asphyxia) is likewise listed as an allowed qualifier complication. Both respiratory failure and asphyxia are closely linked to نقص الأكسجين في الدم (hypoxemia), which is a direct manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية. The mapping of الاِختِنَاق to نقص الأكسجين في الدم highlights that low blood‑oxygen levels are a central pathophysiological feature that underlies these serious complications. Recognizing that sickle cell disease can precipitate severe respiratory compromise and hypoxemic states is essential for effective patient monitoring and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1802
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this hemoglobin disorder commonly manifests as نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating reduced oxygen levels in the blood.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1871
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this disease is clinically associated with قساح. A principal manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (low blood oxygen), which is also mapped to الاِختِنَاق, indicating a link between hypoxemia and asphyxic events. Recognizing that patients with sickle cell anemia (داء الكريات المنجلية) may present with قساح and experience نقص الأكسجين في الدم that can progress to or be interpreted as الاِختِنَاق is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and timely therapeutic intervention.
